coredata.dc:title: A nanocluster-based fluorescent sensor for sensitive hemoglobin detection
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Dongqin
Meng, Huijie
Tu, Yifeng
Yan, Jilin
coredata.dc:description: In this report, a fluorescence sensor for sensitive detection of hemoglobin was developed. Gold nanoclusters were first synthesized with bovine serum albumin. It was found that both hydrogen peroxide and hemoglobin could weakly quench the fluorescence from the gold nanoclusters, but when these two were applied onto the nanolcusters simultaneously, a much improved quenching was resulted. This enhancing effect was proved to come from the catalytic generation of hydroxyl radical by hemoglobin. Under an optimized condition, the quenching linearly related to the concentration of hemoglobin in the range of 1–250nM, and a limit of detection as low as 0.36nM could be obtained. This provided a sensitive means for the quantification of Hb. The sensor was then successfully applied for blood analyses with simple sample pretreatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoclusters
Fluorescence
Quenching
Hemoglobin
Hydrogen peroxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Gold nanocluster and its fluorescence quenching by hemoglobin
Optimization of the detection of hemoglobin
Selectivity and reproducibility of the fluorescence senor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence of the AuNCs (A) and AuNCs mixed with 0.5 μM Hb (B), 200 μM H2O2 (C) and 200μM H2O2 together with 0.5μM Hb (D). The fluorescence measurements were performed with 370nm excitation. The samples have been incubated for 60min under pH 7 citrate-phosphate buffer.

Fluorescence of the AuNCs mixed with 200μM H2O2 together with Hb, Mb, Cyt c and hemin (all set at 1μM), respectively. The fluorescence measurements were performed with 370nm excitation. The samples have been incubated for 60min under pH 7 citrate-phosphate buffer.

Fluorescence intensities of the AuNC with under different pH conditions. The measurements were performed with gold nanocluster together under 20mM citrate-phosphate buffer and 200μM H2O2 together with Hb. The fluorescence signal was collected after 60min incubation with 370nm excitation, and the intensities were collected at 615nm.

Fluorescence intensity of the AuNC with different amount of H2O2. The measurements were performed with gold nanocluster together under 20mM citrate-phosphate buffer (pH 5). The fluorescence signal was collected after 30min incubation with 370nm excitation, and the intensities are collected at 615nm.

(A) Fluorescence responses of the nanocluster towards Hb samples at various concentrations. The measurements were performed with gold nanocluster together with 40μM H2O2 under 20mM citrate-phosphate buffer (pH 5). The fluorescence signals were collected after 30min incubation with 370nm excitation; (B) the Stern-Volmer plot of the fluorescence responses.

Fluorescence responses towards difference species. The concentration of Hb was 0.5μM while all other at 5μM. The measurements were performed with gold nanocluster together with 40μM H2O2 under 20mM citrate-phosphate buffer (pH 5). The fluorescence signal was collected after 30min incubation with 370nm excitation.
Comparison of the analytical performances with different methods.

head.ce:title: A nanocluster-based fluorescent sensor for sensitive hemoglobin detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, 199 Ren’ai Road, Industrial Park, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this report, a fluorescence sensor for sensitive detection of hemoglobin was developed. Gold nanoclusters were first synthesized with bovine serum albumin. It was found that both hydrogen peroxide and hemoglobin could weakly quench the fluorescence from the gold nanoclusters, but when these two were applied onto the nanolcusters simultaneously, a much improved quenching was resulted. This enhancing effect was proved to come from the catalytic generation of hydroxyl radical by hemoglobin. Under an optimized condition, the quenching linearly related to the concentration of hemoglobin in the range of 1–250nM, and a limit of detection as low as 0.36nM could be obtained. This provided a sensitive means for the quantification of Hb. The sensor was then successfully applied for blood analyses with simple sample pretreatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Hemoglobin (Hb) is an iron-containing metalloprotein for the transportation of oxygen in blood red cells [1]. It carries oxygen from respiratory organs to other parts in the body for the further aerobic respiration, which is crucial for the proper function of each part. Hemoglobin is the most abundant protein in human serum and its level is quite important for human health status. Abnormally high or low amount of Hb in human serum can result in some certain healthy issues, such as anemia and polycythemia, respectively. Due to its importance in biochemical and physiological processes, various methods have been developed for Hb measurement, such as electrochemistry [2,3], UV–vis absorption [4,5], luminescence [6–9], liquid chromatography, [10] etc. Among all these methods, the absorption-based ones (with the van Kampen and Zijlstra reagent) are the most popular and are widely adopted in clinical and biological laboratories. However, due to a highly toxic agent (potassium cyanide) required, the process is cumbersome in practical applications [4]. Facile and user- and environment- friendly methods or systems would be welcome for Hb detection.

With the fast developing nanotechnology, researchers are now developing sensing systems with nano-sized materials, such as nanoparticles, nanorods, quantum dots, carbon nanotubes, etc [11]. These systems have been proved capable of providing better performances, such as higher sensitivity, improved selectivity, lower detection limit, etc., as compared with conventional detection systems. Gold metal nanoclusters (AuNCs) are one type of these newly developed nanomaterials [12]. They come with ultra-small sizes and consist only a few to tens of gold atoms in their metallic core [13]. These nanoclusters are usually protected or stabilized by suitable template material, such as dendrimers, proteins, oligonucleotides and peptides [14]. Due to the size approaching their Fermi energy level, extraordinary chemical and physical properties are rendered, one of which is their high luminescence: under suitable irradiations, intensive emission can be obtained from some of the nanoclusters. With this kind of properties, various sensing systems have been developed [15]. These systems are mainly constructed based on the fact that the fluorescence behavior of the AuNCs are highly dependent on their environment, and change of their environmental factors usually results in significant increase or decrease of the fluorescence intensity [16]. For example, their fluorescence can be quenched by quite a few species, such as mercury ion, copper ion, hydrogen peroxide, sulfur ion, etc., and these interactions have been applied in related sensing schemes already [17–19].

In this report, a sensitive detection toward hemoglobin was developed. Protein-stabilized gold nanoclusters were synthesized through a facile method. Although the target species, hemoglobin, functioned as only a weak quencher towards the AuNCs, it was capable of enhancing the quenching from hydrogen peroxide, and this enhancing effect was directly related to the amount of hemoglobin. Thus a sensor for sensitive detection of hemoglobin was developed.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), hydrogen peroxide and ferrous sulfate were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was from Suzhou Yacoo Chemical Co. (Suzhou, China). Human hemoglobin, human serum albumin (HSA), cytochrome C, hemin and a red cell lysis buffer were from Solarbio Technology Co., (Beijing, China)., lysozyme (LZM) from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China), glucose oxidase (GOD) from Yuanye Biotech (Shanghai, China), pepsin were from Boster Bio-tech (Wuhan, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Double-distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

Fluorescence measurements were performed on an F-2500 fluorospectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra were obtained with a TU-1901 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (PGeneral Instruments, China). Microscopic images of the nanoclusters were obtained with a Tecnai G220 transmission electron microscope (TEM, 200kV) (FEI, USA).

Gold nanoclusters were synthesized with bovine serum albumin as both the reducing agent and the template material as previously reported [20–22]. All glassware used was immersed in a chromic acid rinse solution overnight, then thoroughly washed with water. Under vigorous stirring, 5mL 0.01M HAuCl4 was added into 5mL 50mgmL−1 BSA solution. After that, 0.5mL 1M NaOH solution was added, and the so-obtained mixture was incubated on a 37°C water bath for 12h with continuous stirring. Then the water bath and stirring were removed, and the mixture obtained was stored at 4°C in dark before use.

Citrate-phosphate buffers at 20mM were used for controlling the pH value. The stock solution of the gold nanolcusters was diluted 300-fold in all application unless stated otherwise. After a suitable incubation, the fluorescence spectra were recorded on the spectrofluorometer, and intensities at the peak maxima were read for the quantification.

Aliquots of 10μL blood samples (from the Second Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University, Suzhou, China) were first diluted to 100μL with water, then a red cell lysis solution was added to a final volume of 1mL. After 15min incubation, the mixture was centrifuged (RCF 1000×g) for 15min. Then 100μL of the upper layer was applied onto the fluorescence sensor for the measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gold nanocluster and its fluorescence quenching by hemoglobin
ce:section.ce:para: The protocol from Xie et al. provides a facile means for the synthesis of protein-stabilized gold nanoclusters [20]. Following that protocol, bovine serum albumin (BSA), a highly abundant protein was adopted. BSA here worked as the stabilizer, which bonded with the gold through the sulfur containing amino acid cysteine [20]. Under the transmission electronic microscope (TEM), the BSA-AuNCs obtained here were found with diameter around 2nm (Fig. S1 in the Supplementary material). The nanoclusters absorbed with absorption maxima around 370nm, and a red luminescence with maximum at 615nm is provided (Fig. S2).

Previously it is already known that some certain metal ions, such as copper, mercury and iron etc. might change the fluorescence of AuNCs [21–23]. As an iron-containing protein, it is possible that Hb might influence the luminescence behavior of the AuNCs [23]. In order to evaluate this influence, AuNCs and Hb were first directly mixed. It showed that the AuNCs fluorescence decreased as expected, which might come from the reduction of Au(I) in the metallic core of the AuNCs into Au(0) by the divalent iron in the heme part of the Hb [24]. However, this kind of interaction was not significant. For example, as compared with the intact AuNCs (curve a in Fig. 1), a Hb sample at 0.5μM gave out only 2.5% of fluorescence decrease of the AuNCs (curve b in Fig. 1). This meant if this direct interaction scheme was to be applied for the quantification of Hb, only limited sensitivity could be obtained. In order to evaluate this kind direct interaction, Hb samples at different concentrations were tested. It was found the extent of quenching (F0-F)/F0 (in which F0 and F were fluorescence intensity at the emission maxima before and after of the nanocluster before and after the introduction of Hb, respectively) was linearly related to the concentration of Hb in the range of 0.5–8μM (Fig. S3), and a limite of detection was estimated at 0.1μM (three times the standard deviation of the background noise, 3σ). This direct reaction scheme only provided limited sensitivity, and some improvement should be made.

As a strong oxidant, hydrogen peroxide is capable of breaking the gold–sulfur bond between the template material (eg. BSA here) and the metallic core of the cluster, and renders some kind of fluorescence quenching [18]. This phenomenon has been coupled to various enzymatic reaction for related analysis, such as with the help of glucose oxidase and urate oxidase for glucose and uric acid detections, respectively [18,19,25]. However, the quenching effect from H2O2 was neither significant, which usually limited its detection at micromolar level only [19]. Here a hydrogen peroxide sample at 200μM mixed with AuNC gave out a 12% decrease of the fluorescence (curve c in Fig. 1).

In the case where Hb together with hydrogen peroxide was applied onto AuNCs, a much enhanced quenching was obtained. As illustrated in Fig. 1, when samples at the same concentration were applied (200μM hydrogen peroxide and 0.5μM hemoglobin), a 49% decrease of the intensity was obtained as compared with intact AuNCs (curve d in Fig. 1). This extent of quenching was much greater than the simple sum of the fluorescence decrease from hydrogen peroxide alone (12%) and Hb alone (2.5%). As seen from this result, the improved quenching was not simply the accumulation of the two factors. Another variation in the fluorescence was the shift of the peak maxima from 615nm to 605nm, which indicated the change of the AuNCs as well.

As previously reported, the quenching of AuNCs fluorescence from hydrogen peroxide is due to the interaction between hydrogen peroxide and the gold–sulfur (Au–S) bond inside the nanoclusters [26], which is crucial for the stability of the nanocluster. Although hydrogen peroxide is one of the strongest oxidative species [27], under suitable conditions it may be converted into even stronger products. One example is the Fenton's reagent, in which ferrous ion (Fe2+) combines with hydrogen peroxide and forms even more oxidative hydroxyl and hydroperoxyl radicals [28,29]. Thus an enhancement of the fluorescence quenching is obtained during the interaction with the AuNCs.
(1) Fe2++H2O2→Fe3++HO•+OH−
(2) Fe3++H2O2→Fe2++HOO•+H+

In order to confirm the proposed scheme for the Hb-related reaction, some additional experiments were performed. Ferrous ion and Hb were tested. It was found that as both two weak quenchers to BSA-AuNC fluorescence, they presented similar enhancements of the H2O2-based quenching (Fig. S4). To further demonstrate the effect, thiourea (TU), known as a hydroxyl-radical scavenger capable of removing the active hydroxyl radicals, was introduced into the reaction [30]. As seen from the results (Fig. S4), although itself a quencher toward AuNCs (which might come from the interaction of sulfur inside molecule with gold in the AuNCs), when it was added into the hydrogen peroxide or hydrogen peroxide together with Fe2+ or Hb, some recovery of the fluorescence was obtained. This suggested that the proposed radical-based pathway might be the source of the Hb related quenching.

Because this kind of improvement on quenching came from the iron-containing part inside Hb, it was reasonable that molecules with a similar composition might have similar responses. Some other species with heme structure were tested. It was found that when set at the same concentration (1μM), Hb gave out the greatest quenching, followed by myoglobin ( Fig. 2). Cytochrome C rendered a weak quenching similar to that of hemin [31]. The higher quenching efficiency of Hb might come from the greater amount of heme inside its structure: each Hb molecule contains four heme subgroups [24]. This verified the functionality heme from another aspect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the detection of hemoglobin
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain satisfactory responses toward hemoglobin, the experimental conditions for the sensor were optimized. First, as the pH value was important for both the AuNCs fluorescence and its quenching, signals under different pH conditions were compared. The greatest quenching was obtained at pH 5.0, which was then adopted for followed experiment ( Fig. 3). The kinetics of the quenching behavior was evaluated as well. It showed that the fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly in the first a few minutes, and remained almost unchanged thereafter ( Fig. 4A). In order to avoid a poor reproducibility (fast decrease) from the first few minutes, all following detections were conducted after 30min reaction.

The concentration of hydrogen peroxide mattered as well. Hydrogen peroxide solutions at different concentrations were prepared and tested with the presence of Hb at the same concentration (0.5μM). Increasing of the H2O2 concentration resulted in greater quenching. However, this tendency was only obvious in the case below 40μM, and the contribution from even higher concentration of H2O2 was almost negligible. A final concentration of 40μM was used (Fig. 4B).

Under the optimized conditions, the relationship between the concentration of Hb and the quenching was studied ( Fig. 5). It was found that the extent of the fluorescence quenching was linearly related to Hb concentration in the range of 1–250nM (with correlation coefficient R2 as 0.9958). The limit of detection towards Hb was estimated at 0.36nM (3σ), which was more than two orders of magnitude lower than the value from the Hb direct quenching scheme (0.1μM). This performance is better than or comparable to similar reported values ( Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and reproducibility of the fluorescence senor
ce:section.ce:para: As Hb's existing in a complicated blood matrix, the influence from the possible coexisting components was evaluated. Species such as some common ions (K+, Na+ Ca2+, Mg2+), glucose, urea, uric acid (UA), cysteine (cys), human serum albumin (HSA), lysozyme (LZM), glucose oxidase (GOD), etc. did not render any significant change of the fluorescence here, which meant they do not interfere with the analysis ( Fig. 6). Pepsin was the only substance that gave some minor quenching, which might due to its capability of degrading the nanocluster through hydrolysis of BSA [34]. In a reproducibility study, a relative standard deviation of 1.38% was obtained from seven consecutive measurement of a Hb sample at 150nM, which indicated a pretty good reproducibility of the developed sensor.

From the previous results, the high performances of the AuNC-based sensor enabled related applications into real sample analysis. Also owing to the high sensitivity and low detection limit, large-fold dilution could be adopted in sample pretreatment, which further reduced the possible interferences. Blood samples were pretreated, and after a simple dilution, they were directly analyzed with the developed sensor. The results obtained were in good accordance with the normal range of Hb concentration. In a recovery study, suitable amount of Hb standard was added and recoveries within the range of 98.0–100.2% were obtained, indicating the reliability of the developed sensor ( Table 2).

In this work, an enhancing of the gold nanocluster fluorescence quenching by hydrogen peroxide was found with hemoglobin. This effect was directly related to the amount of Hb and thus a sensitive fluorescence sensor for the detection of hemoglobin down to sub-nanomolarsub-nanomolar was developed. For the high performances of the nanocluster-based sensor, only simple dilution was required for blood sample treatment. The sensor could be adopted in clinical and other applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21305100), the Project of Scientific and Technologic Infrastructure of Suzhou (SZS20120) and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.03.095.
